interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SNCC Field Secretary; Civic Interest Group Treasurer; Cambridge (Maryland) Movement Organizer
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

main_summary.summary:
Reginald "Reggie" Robinson, born in Baltimore, Maryland, on October 2, 1939, recounts his journey from a Baltimore teenager who left Dunbar High School in the tenth grade to a SNCC field secretary who helped open Mississippi voter-registration work in McComb and led SNCC's Cambridge, Maryland project. After dropping out of high school and drifting on the streets, his cousin Josephine Thorn convinced him to enroll at Cortez Peters Business College, where dean Walter Dixon (also a Baltimore City Councilman who introduced a public accommodations bill) drew him into the student Civic Interest Group around 1960, just after the Greensboro sit-ins. Robinson rose to treasurer of CIG and ran voter-registration drives in Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s Baltimore district, registering thousands. When Clarence Mitchell III stepped aside in 1961, Robinson took Maryland's seat at the SNCC meeting in Louisville, Kentucky, where Ella Baker recognized him as the nephew of her colleague "McKinley."

Dispatched to Mississippi in mid-1961, Robinson traveled through Atlanta — where SCLC executive director Wyatt Tee Walker tried to recruit him — then on to McComb to join Bob Moses. Working with C.C. Bryant, Webb Owens ("Super Cool Daddy"), and local supporters like Mama Cotton, he set up bank accounts, rented mimeograph equipment over Burglund Supermarket, and helped run a citizenship/literacy school teaching Mississippi residents to interpret the state constitution for voter registration. He describes visiting Curtis Hayes, Hollis Watkins, and other jailed Pike County student demonstrators at the Magnolia jail dressed in suit and tie. After Herbert Lee was killed by State Representative E.H. Hurst at the cotton gin and the McComb high school students walked out, Robinson helped shield Diane Nash and businessman A.G. Gaston on the roof of the Masonic Temple while Bob Moses, Charles Sherrod, and Cordell Reagon were beaten and arrested. Following the McComb retreat, he was sent to coordinate Route 40 work with CORE and then to Cambridge, Maryland, where after a Crisfield arrest he helped build the local SNCC affiliate around Freddie St. Clair and later Gloria Richardson. There he negotiated with Maryland National Guard General George Gelston, confronted Attorney General Robert Kennedy, and pressed Richardson against a public-accommodations referendum boycott he saw as betraying SNCC's southern voter-registration sacrifices.

Robinson recounts close working relationships with SNCC chairman Chuck McDew and executive secretary James Forman, his Voter Education Project work in Orangeburg, South Carolina with State College support, and a brief Albany, Georgia stint delivering food to Martin Luther King Jr. and Ralph Abernathy in jail at Wyatt Tee Walker's request. In 1964 he campaigned for Senator Joseph D. Tydings, then lobbied California and Oregon for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP), worked support at the Atlantic City convention with Mary Lovelace O'Neal, did special-gift fundraising in New York, and was reassigned to Philadelphia before McDew called him out of the field at the end of 1965. After SNCC he worked for the United Planning Organization in Washington with McDew, then for the Child Development Group of Mississippi — losing that job after challenging Senator John C. Stennis's repeated address of "you boys." Later jobs took him to Detroit (OSTI), Oakland (Kaiser Engineers Cor Re East Oakland housing project), Memphis (A.L. Nellum & Associates Black Caucus dinner), and the District of Columbia government, where he retired in May 2001 after twenty years as an unemployment-insurance investigator and assistant LAN administrator.

In closing, Robinson reflects on Ella Baker's bottom-up organizing philosophy, his friendships with Joanne Grant, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Diane Nash, his Peekskill rest stay arranged by psychiatrist Joyce Baker leading to a sauna encounter with Paul Robeson at Sam Rosen's Katonah, New York home, and his marriage at Harry Belafonte's home with his mother and Ella Baker in attendance. He criticizes Oprah Winfrey's Selma film and praises Phil Alden Robinson's Freedom Song for accurately depicting SNCC's organizing in Mississippi. His central call is for Black entrepreneurs to pool capital and build community financial institutions, laundromats, restaurants, and electronics factories in small towns like Greenwood, Mississippi — arguing that voter registration alone, without an economic base, has reached its limit. He defines himself as a Free Black Person (FBP) since 1961 and signs his emails accordingly.
key_themes: Bottom-up Ella Baker organizing philosophy and SNCC's build-and-leave model
Baltimore-Cortez Peters Business College entry point into the Civic Interest Group
Mississippi voter registration in McComb and Pike County alongside Bob Moses
Cambridge Maryland Movement — Gloria Richardson, public-accommodations referendum, National Guard occupation
Black economic power as the unfinished work of the civil rights movement
SNCC's organizational culture: chairman Chuck McDew, executive secretary James Forman, Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, Julian Bond communications
Personal mentorship by Ella Baker (mother figure) and friendships with women in SNCC (Diane Nash, Joanne Grant, Fannie Lou Hamer, Casey Hayden)
main_summary.historical_significance: Robinson's interview is a first-person field-secretary account of two understudied SNCC fronts: the original 1961 McComb voter-registration opening alongside Bob Moses and the 1962-64 Cambridge, Maryland project — one of the few movement battlegrounds outside the deep South where the National Guard was called out and the federal government negotiated public accommodations. His detailed reconstruction of Pike County jail visits, the McComb high school walkout, and the Mama Cotton boarding house under Bob Moses contributes texture to events that historians know primarily from Bob Moses and Hollis Watkins. His insider testimony about Ella Baker's recognition of him at the 1961 Louisville SNCC meeting, his work confronting Senator John C. Stennis at CDGM, and his post-SNCC trajectory through urban Black economic development projects (UPO, OSTI, Kaiser Engineers Oakland, Marion Barry's DC government) extends the SNCC record into the post-1965 era of community development work that the movement generation largely carried on its own.

errors: The summary identifies Clarence Mitchell III's grandmother as president of the NAACP. The transcript states only 'Clarence's grandmother had said that the NAACP, she was president of the NAACP' (segment 67). Historical record identifies Lillie Mae Carroll Jackson as Clarence Mitchell III's grandmother and Baltimore NAACP branch president (not national NAACP president); the summary preserves the transcript phrasing but a publication editor should clarify Baltimore-chapter vs. national.

The transcript names the McComb cab driver as 'George Hitz' (segments 169-249) but ASR likely renders 'George Hayes' (the documented McComb local who drove SNCC workers). Summary uses 'George Hayes' per likely Whisper variant; should be verified against original audio.

Transcript at segment 354 names businessman as 'A.G. Gaston' but historical record places A.G. Gaston in Birmingham, not McComb. This may be a Whisper mistranscription of a different local name; summary repeats the transcript's claim and would need ground-truth verification before publication.

Transcript spells the SCLC pre-recruiter as 'Ed King' (segments 144-166), identified as SNCC executive director. The historically documented SNCC executive director at that time was Edward B. King Jr. (a different person from Rev. Edwin King the white Mississippi minister). Summary preserves the role-correct 'Ed King.' No fabrication, but disambiguation needed.

The summary's reference to 'Cor Re East Oakland' for the Kaiser Engineers housing project derives from transcript 'correct in East Oakland' (segment 1322) which is likely a Whisper mistranscription of a place name (possibly 'Corregidor' or similar). Summary should not assert a specific project name without verification.
improvements: Editor pass should disambiguate the two 'Ed King's of the Movement (SNCC's Edward B. King Jr. vs. Rev. Edwin King of MFDP) before publication.
Cross-check transcript 'George Hitz' and 'A.G. Gaston' against the McComb SNCC chronology in Watkins/Moses oral histories.
Specify the Baltimore vs. national NAACP scope of Lillie Mae Carroll Jackson's presidency.
Replace 'Cor Re' with the actual Oakland neighborhood name once verified.
Summary correctly omits embellishment of dates Robinson himself flagged as uncertain (he repeatedly self-corrects on years).

chapters.title: Baltimore Childhood, Family, and Cortez Peters Business College
chapters.summary: Robinson opens the interview by stating he was born in Baltimore, Maryland, on October 2, 1939, to Leroy and Leo Vester Robinson (mother's maiden name Jenkins). He had an older brother Leroy and a sister Yula Jean, with Reggie being the youngest. His father worked at Bethlehem Steel for 39 years and died one month after retiring; his mother did housework and piecework starting when Reggie reached first grade. Robinson attended Dunbar High School through about tenth and a half grades before deciding French wasn't going to help him and 'the streets won.' After a job fell apart, his cousin Josephine Thorn — a Social Security supervisor — convinced him and his father to enroll him at Cortez Peters Business College, a Black-founded business school (one of three in Washington, Baltimore, and Chicago) that taught government-test preparation, shorthand, business law, accounting, typing, and mimeograph stencil work.

improvements: Cortez Peters Business College detail (typewriter sponsor 'royal or underwear typewriter') is preserved with appropriate uncertainty in the transcript; summary correctly elides the brand.

chapters.title: Civic Interest Group, Voter Registration, and the Path to SNCC
chapters.summary: After the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, the Civic Interest Group (CIG) student group formed in Baltimore, drawing on Morgan State and other Maryland colleges with Morgan as the 'head Hancho.' Walter Dixon, Robinson's school dean and a Baltimore City Councilman who had introduced a public accommodations bill, sent Robinson to represent Cortez Peters at the joint college meetings. Robinson worked his way into the group as it shifted from direct action to voter registration, raising about 30,000 voters with sound-trucks and school-bus rallies in Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s congressional district. Because he had some accounting background, he became CIG's treasurer. At the June 1961 meeting, Clarence Mitchell III reported that SNCC was preparing to combine voter registration and direct action; Mitchell's grandmother (president of the NAACP) had said the NAACP would no longer bail out the students, forcing Clarence to choose family over SNCC. Maryland's seat at SNCC opened up, and Robinson volunteered. The night before he left for Louisville he got hit in the eye with a pretzel and traveled wearing a black eye-patch.

errors: Summary follows transcript characterization of 'Clarence's grandmother' as 'president of the NAACP' (segment 67). Historical record: Lillie Mae Carroll Jackson was president of the Baltimore NAACP branch, not the national NAACP. Transcript is the source of this potential conflation.
improvements: Clarify Baltimore-chapter vs. national NAACP presidency for Lillie Mae Carroll Jackson.

chapters.title: Louisville SNCC Meeting, Meeting Ella Baker, and Baltimore Rally
chapters.summary: Robinson recounts arriving at the SNCC meeting in Louisville, Kentucky (June 1961) wearing a black eye-patch — everyone assumed he'd been injured in battle, and he didn't correct them. He felt outside the group until he was standing alone on Anne Braden's porch with a beer when a small woman approached, asked about his family connections — Smithfield, Virginia father; mother Leo Vester Jenkins; uncle McKinley — and announced 'I know who you are. You're just like him. He works with me in New York.' This was Ella Baker, who knew his uncle and his church-active Baltimore aunts. Baker's recognition opened doors with Chuck McDew and the SNCC voice. Robinson then secured the next SNCC meeting for Baltimore, where King was the noted speaker coming to dissuade SNCC from going into Mississippi. Robinson convinced everyone to collect the offering at the Masonic Temple before the SCLC ministers arrived — splitting the proceeds between CIG and SNCC. When Wyatt Tee Walker reached for the collection moment in his speech, someone whispered that 'the collection has been done.'

errors: Transcript renders the SNCC meeting timing ambiguously: 'July of, you know, I went in June of 61' (segments 76-77) — Robinson self-corrects to June. Summary uses June.
improvements: Anne Braden's porch in Louisville is a documented SCEF gathering location; preserved appropriately.

chapters.title: Atlanta Layover, Arrival in McComb, and Building SNCC Infrastructure
chapters.summary: Robinson describes leaving Baltimore by train for Atlanta, where he was met by SNCC executive director Ed King — fresh from integrating the National Symphony of Atlanta and still in top hat and tails. Ed King introduced Robinson around the SCLC offices on Auburn Avenue, where Wyatt Tee Walker tried again to recruit him for SCLC. New orders sent Robinson to McComb (not the Delta) to meet Bob Moses. From New Orleans he took the Illinois Central to McComb, where local cab driver George Hayes met him and took him to C.C. Bryant's house to join Bob Moses. Robinson was introduced to Webb Owens (known locally as 'Super Cool Daddy'), who walked him through the practical setup: housing at Mama Cotton's on Wall Street, meals at Mr. Plevis's restaurant and Miss Quinn's, a SNCC bank account in town, and rental of a mimeograph machine, stencils, and white-out from the local stationery store. Doing business with white McComb merchants was a deliberate strategy. They set up a school over Burglund Supermarket in the Masonic Temple, where Robinson canvassed in the projects (briefly fearing white sewer workmen were tracking him) and began teaching residents to read and interpret the Mississippi Constitution — required to register to vote.

errors: Transcript renders the cab driver 'George Hitz' (segments 169-249) — likely Whisper rendering of 'George Hayes,' a documented McComb SNCC driver. Summary uses 'George Hayes' as the likely canonical form.

Transcript names 'Ed King' as SNCC executive director (segments 144-166). The documented SNCC executive director at that time was Edward B. King Jr. (a different person from Rev. Edwin King of Mississippi). Disambiguation needed for publication.
improvements: Cross-check 'Hitz' vs. 'Hayes' against McComb chronology.
Disambiguate the two 'Ed King's of the Movement.

chapters.title: Pike County Direct Action and the Magnolia Jail Visit
chapters.summary: Robinson describes how the McComb voter-registration school was followed by student direct action: Curtis Hayes, Hollis Watkins, Bobby Talbert, Brenda Travis, and others sat in at the bus station and were arrested. Bob Moses argued SNCC should support the students by visiting them in jail and leaving them long enough for PR before bail. Robinson was elected to visit. With George Hayes driving (and producing a silver-handled pistol from the seat for the trip down to Magnolia), Robinson arrived in a black suit looking 'like a lawyer or a minister' with a briefcase. He confronted the tobacco-chewing jailer who initially refused — 'I will not let you up in here' — but eventually buzzed him through three slamming doors to the cell. He delivered messages, took notes, and on leaving called 'jailer' to be released. He reported back through Julian Bond that everyone was holding firm. Robinson notes he did not see the female arrestees including Brenda Travis on this visit.

improvements: Robinson's description of the briefcase-and-suit jail-visit strategy aligns with documented SNCC field practice.

chapters.title: Baltimore Sit-ins, Backers, and the Decision to Go South
chapters.summary: Robinson reflects on the Baltimore demonstrations: as an East Baltimore kid in a West Baltimore movement, he had to prove himself. At the peak of restaurant-association sit-ins, the group piled seven to eight people into each of three Renaults and rotated through Baltimore restaurants while police read the Miranda Act. The NAACP urged them to stop because the public accommodations bill was in the courts. William 'Little Willie' Adams — whose wife served in the Maryland House of Delegates — quietly helped with bail through his bondsman, Dixon. South Baltimore and Little Italy sit-ins turned uglier than the rest of the city, but nothing matched McComb or Atlanta lunch counters. Robinson credits Mrs. Elizabeth Murphy Oliver of the Baltimore Afro-American newspaper, her daughter Marcia, AFL-CIO union representative Troy Brailey (who knew Robinson's father), and Larry Steele (Jet/Ebony stringer who checked in on Robinson's mother) as personal supporters. When the call came for who would commit fully to the southern work, Robinson signed on — reasoning the danger was no worse than what he had already faced in Baltimore. His father was proud; his mother was 'as cool as moms could be.'

improvements: William 'Little Willie' Adams was indeed a prominent Baltimore Black businessman and political backer; reference preserved correctly.

chapters.title: The McComb High School Walkout, Herbert Lee, and Retreat to Atlanta
chapters.summary: Robinson describes hearing the high school students marching on the morning Diane Nash was hiding from a warrant — Bob Moses had arrived the night before despite the SNCC field workers' fears that a 'white boy' on the march would worsen the violence. The walkout led to general arrest: Bob Moses was beaten so badly that other SNCC workers covered his body. Robinson, posted as an observer in the Masonic Temple, saw Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon picked up by police; then a large police sergeant began climbing the stairs toward the temple where Diane Nash and 'A.G. Gaston of Detroit' (per transcript) were hidden. Robinson led both through a black cloth at the back of the Masonic Temple and onto the roof, where the officer searched all around the temple without finding them. Gaston, unable to wait, jumped from the roof and was caught on a highway. Robinson got Diane Nash back inside, then to Wall Street. The group withdrew to Atlanta, where they learned Herbert Lee had been killed in the cotton gin by Mississippi State Representative E.H. Hurst. Robinson had the assignment of photographing Louis Allen at the subsequent mass meeting/funeral, which Amzie Moore convinced them to hold to show they were not afraid. Allen was later murdered. The retreat to Atlanta scattered SNCC fieldworkers and triggered James Forman's arrival, which Robinson credits with bringing organizational structure to the operation.
related_events: James Forman becomes SNCC executive secretary

errors: Transcript identifies 'A.G. Gaston' as a Detroit businessman hidden with Diane Nash on the Masonic Temple roof (segment 354). Historically A.G. Gaston was a Birmingham businessman, not Detroit. This is either a transcript identifier conflation, a Whisper transcription error, or Robinson's misremembering. Summary preserves transcript phrasing but flags for review.
Herbert Lee's killer is correctly identified as Mississippi State Representative E.H. Hurst (matches civil_rights_facts.json entry on Herbert Lee).
improvements: Verify 'A.G. Gaston of Detroit' against actual McComb businessman roster — likely a different person.
Photograph-of-Louis-Allen-at-mass-meeting detail is unique testimony worth surfacing.

chapters.title: Cambridge Maryland Movement: Crisfield Arrest, Gloria Richardson, and Bill Hansen
chapters.summary: After McComb, Robinson was assigned to coordinate Route 40 work with CORE. A call from Ruby Doris Smith Robinson sent him to the CORE office where a group from New York — Faith Holsaert, Peggy Damman, Angela Butler, and others, joined by Bill Hansen (who had just demonstrated in Annapolis) and local Baltimore folk — was preparing a sit-in at Governor Tawes's restaurant in Crisfield, Maryland at Christmas 1961. They were arrested after the Miranda reading and held in Princess Anne. The Mitchell family again provided attorneys; bondsmen Walter Black (Eastern Shore) and Freddie St. Clair (Cambridge) bailed them out. Freddie St. Clair invited SNCC to Cambridge, and Bill Hansen became field secretary there. The Civic Interest Group, NAG, and other SNCC affiliates rotated weekend demonstrators into Cambridge. Robinson clashed with Hansen, whom he saw as 'hiding from his own community' and unnecessarily provocative — particularly Hansen's weekend sit-ins at the Choptank Inn where he was repeatedly thrown through windows. When Freddie St. Clair's family funeral business pulled him out of leadership, his cousin Gloria Richardson stepped in. Richardson came from an old Cambridge slaveholding-merchant family lineage. The board around Richardson grew strong enough to negotiate with the National Guard, Kennedy administration, and federal HUD officials.

errors: Bill Hansen's documented role per civil_rights_facts.json was SNCC Arkansas Project director 1962-67; transcript places him at SNCC Cambridge Maryland project before Arkansas, which is consistent with the 1962-63 chronology.
improvements: Robinson's Hansen critique is unfiltered first-person testimony — preserve verbatim quality.

chapters.title: Cambridge Negotiations, National Guard, and the Referendum Showdown
chapters.summary: Robinson recounts the Cambridge Movement's escalation. After clashes with Bill Hansen's Arkansas-bound style, Robinson moved through Selma briefly — meeting Amelia Boynton and 12 ministers ('the 12 high') — and was credited with the early reconnaissance work that brought Bernard Lafayette to Selma. In Cambridge, the white establishment proposed a public accommodations referendum and Gloria Richardson said no Black folks would vote — Robinson pushed back hard, arguing SNCC affiliates could not advocate non-voting while southern SNCC workers were dying for the franchise. Maryland sent in Adjutant General George Gelston of the National Guard, who became an unlikely ally. Robinson and Richardson, with lawyer Fred Weisgal, met at Weisgal's Baltimore home to plan strategy. Robinson recounts the absurd episode of Gelston offering him an officer's commission, only to discover Robinson was 4-F mentally incompetent because at his draft physical he insisted ink-blots were ink-blots. When Gelston was out of town, his subordinate Colonel Tawes (the governor's nephew) faced a confrontation where Cliff Voss charged the Guard's fixed bayonets; tear gas was deployed across the Black community. Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox, and others were arrested. The Guard later integrated when Colonel Boxer, a Black officer from Baltimore, arrived as second in command. Bobby Kennedy then engaged more directly, and HUD secretary Robert C. Weaver made promises Robinson considers largely unfulfilled.

errors: Transcript renders Robert Kennedy quote 'I would give you one, but you know where I tie' (segments 580-583) — a likely Whisper rendering of 'you know what I'd tie' or similar. Summary preserves the spirit of the Kennedy meeting without quoting the garbled phrase.

Robinson's claim about being the SNCC point of contact establishing Selma's 12-minister network (segments 466-470) and feeding Bernard Lafayette into Selma is first-person testimony; should be cross-checked against Lafayette's own oral history.
improvements: Verify Robinson's pre-Lafayette Selma reconnaissance against Lafayette's published account.
Verify Colonel Boxer name and rank against Maryland National Guard records.

chapters.title: Cambridge Wrap-Up, NAACP Tensions, and SNCC Office Politics
chapters.summary: Robinson reflects on Gloria Richardson's leadership: not charismatic but responsive to her board, which she met with for direction; the St. Clair family name and her own old-Cambridge background made the Cambridge movement what it was. The NAACP's position — 'we have it in court' — clashed with SNCC's direct action push. Henry G. Parks Jr. provided critical financial support after the NAACP withdrew. Robinson recounts how Norma (who came out of Morgan with leaders Clarence Logan, Phil Savage, and Levin West) joined SNCC as a secretary as voter-registration record-keeping demanded it. August Meier of Morgan was among the adult advisors alongside Reverend Marion Bascom, Reverend Vernon Dobson, Reverend Frank Williams, and 'Katherine Adams' as secretary to the advisory group. Robinson recalls being annoyed at the March on Washington when SNCC's planned speech was 'bumped' by SCLC's preferences ('the power is win'). He briefly describes John Lewis being elected SNCC chairman in part because Ella Baker chose him as the only candidate not lobbying for it, the rest divided by Nashville-Atlanta-NAG rivalries.

errors: Robinson's account of John Lewis's election as Ella Baker's selection because he wasn't lobbying (segments 982-996) is one perspective; multiple SNCC oral histories give different accounts — the summary attributes the view to Robinson without endorsing it.
improvements: Norma's full name is not given in transcript; the historical SNCC office secretary 'Norma Collins' could be the referent but should not be asserted without verification.

chapters.title: Sisters in the Movement, Mrs. Cotton's Boarding House, and SNCC's Underworld Cambridge
chapters.summary: Robinson lavishly remembers the women of SNCC: meeting Diane Nash for the first time at the Louisville meeting, doodling hearts on his note pad that Connie Curry teased him about; protecting Nash during the McComb walkout; Casey Hayden; Bobbie Yancey learning to fundraise; Sherie Cooks; Ruby Doris Smith Robinson 'Big Mama'; Chessie Johnson managing money; Brenda Travis; Mrs. Cotton — whose McComb boarding house, the SNCC team later learned, was a 'whore house' but provided the only roof willing to take them in. He praises Gwen Greene 'Hill' (married Bill Withers), Grace Brooks from South Carolina State who joined the Free Southern Theater, and reflects on what Robinson calls 'the underworld' opening up the Cambridge Maryland movement: pool-hall regulars and frequently-jailed men were the first to join the demonstrations, ahead of respectable Black Cambridge.

errors: Transcript renders 'Gwen Green' marrying 'Bill Withers' (segment 1076) — the singer Bill Withers married Marcia Johnson in 1976, not a SNCC field worker. This appears to be either Robinson's misremembering or a Whisper conflation. Summary preserves transcript content but flags for caution.
Mrs. Cotton boarding house 'whore house' detail is preserved as Robinson's own characterization.
improvements: Verify Gwen Greene's actual spouse — likely not the singer Bill Withers.
'Bobbi Yancey' may be Bobbie Yancey — verify spelling.

chapters.title: Post-SNCC Career: UPO, CDGM, Kaiser Oakland, and DC Government
chapters.summary: Robinson left SNCC in late 1965 when Chuck McDew called him out of the field. McDew, then director of United Planning Organization Center Number One in Washington, brought Robinson on as second in command; they ran the center for about a year before being pushed out politically. Ed Brown then recruited Robinson to the Child Development Group of Mississippi (CDGM) as Social Service Director. He lost that job at a CDGM meeting with Senator John C. Stennis after asking why Stennis kept prefacing remarks with 'you boys' when 'ain't a damn boy in this room.' Noel Day (married to Peggy Damman) brought him into OSTI, where he worked the Detroit east-side housing project with the Free Detroit Committee. Then Noel sent him to Oakland to work with Kaiser Engineers and the Black Panthers on a 40-year housing acquisition plan (the East Oakland 'Cor Re' project — likely a Whisper rendering of a specific neighborhood). The Panthers rejected him for riding around in a 'steel-grade Cadillac' on Kaiser's tab; Tim Jenkins introduced him to Don Warden, and they reorganized as a community group that did acquire the land for what became a senior-citizens project. After a personal crisis around back pain, smoking, and marriage difficulties, Bobbi Yancey called him for an A.L. Nellum & Associates Black Caucus dinner project in Memphis. He moved to DC permanently in 1981. Stints followed at Pride, a youth division of the DC Department of Employment Services under Marion Barry's summer youth program (running 40,000 summer jobs and 25,000 school-year jobs), an unemployment-insurance investigator job, and finally Assistant LAN Administrator. He retired May 2001 — twenty years to the day after starting — leaving abruptly after a mandatory motivational-speaker break-up during 'elbows-deep' work that he saw as bullshit. He became a 'free black person' (FBP), the goal he says he set in 1961.

errors: Transcript renders the Oakland project as 'correct in East Oakland' (segment 1322) — almost certainly a Whisper mistranscription of a neighborhood or project name. Summary acknowledges uncertainty in the description.
'A.L. Nellum & Associates' detail (segment 1511) preserved from transcript; consultancy is documented but not extensively in public sources.
improvements: Verify Oakland Kaiser project actual name; consult Kaiser Engineers historical records if available.
Confirm Marion Barry summer youth program numbers (40,000 / 25,000) against DC Department of Employment Services records.

chapters.title: 1964 MFDP Atlantic City, Special-Gift Fundraising, and Albany Jail Visit
chapters.summary: When asked about summer 1964, Robinson recounts he left the field briefly to work for Senator Joseph D. Tydings (with Ella Baker's blessing — 'if you feel as though he's going to advance you in understanding what's going on, go ahead'). After the Tydings campaign he was assigned to lobby California and Oregon for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP) before the Atlantic City convention. In Atlantic City he worked support — locating housing — with Mary Lovelace O'Neal of NAG; an Atlantic City underworld figure named Reggie Edgel helped them get hotel rooms for MFDP delegates. Robinson then moved to New York for special-gift fundraising, then to the Philadelphia office until Fred Mealy 'fucked up' and the gains were lost. James Forman pulled him out toward a Texas fundraising assignment. He describes a separate Albany, Georgia stint: visiting Albany Movement jailed activists with Jim Forman, then later (during the SCLC campaign) being asked by Wyatt Tee Walker to take food trays into the jail where Martin Luther King Jr. and Ralph Abernathy (nicknamed 'Old Pork Chops') were held in silk pajamas. After Selma's later commemorations he was introduced by Bob Moses as 'the first SNCC field worker who landed in Selma' (a claim he himself questions). He stayed touch with Bob Moses but did not work on the Algebra Project's Saturday programs.

errors: Bob Moses identifying Robinson as 'first SNCC field worker who landed in Selma' (segment 1823) — Robinson himself qualifies this as something he questions; the documented Selma reconnaissance role is generally credited to Bernard Lafayette and Bob Moses. Summary preserves Robinson's self-skepticism.
improvements: Mary Lovelace O'Neal is correctly identified as a NAG (Nonviolent Action Group, Howard) member who became a celebrated painter; her Atlantic City role is appropriate per transcript.

chapters.title: Ella Baker, Fannie Lou Hamer, Paul Robeson, and Personal Mentorship
chapters.summary: Robinson describes the close mother-figure relationship with Ella Baker, mediated by his own mother. When he was changing assignments from the Tydings campaign to MFDP lobbying, Miss Hamer visited DC and Robinson got Hamer and Baker together at his mother's house for lunch. Ella Baker and Joanne Grant took him out drinking the night before his marriage at Harry Belafonte's home. During the Cambridge work, when Robinson was burned out, Baker suggested he see Dr. Joyce Baker of the Medical Committee for Human Rights, who arranged a week's rest in Peekskill, New York at a Paul Robeson commemorative fundraiser featuring John Hammond Jr. He was then taken to Dr. Sam and Helen Rosen's home in Katonah, New York. (Dr. Sam Rosen was the physician who traveled to Russia to bring Paul Robeson home.) Robinson found himself in the sauna and natural cold stream with Paul Robeson himself. He returned to Cambridge revitalized and began planning his exit from Gloria Richardson's project — Richardson having petitioned Atlanta unsuccessfully to keep him. Robinson got rid of a corrupt 'silk-suit vs. denim' surrounding group (with help from reporter Carl Rogoff) and chased the NAACP element out of town.

errors: Dr. Sam Rosen is the famous American otologist who developed a hearing operation; he was a friend of Paul Robeson and entertained him at Katonah but the 'went to Russia to bring back Paul Robeson' detail is preserved per transcript without historical verification. Robeson did travel to and from Russia in the late 1950s after his passport was restored in 1958.
Dr. Joyce Baker is preserved per transcript; the historical Medical Committee for Human Rights had multiple physicians on staff.
improvements: Verify Dr. Sam Rosen's specific role in Robeson's late-life return from the Soviet Union.
John Hammond Jr.'s role at the Peekskill fundraiser is a detailed and verifiable claim worth checking.

chapters.title: Reflections on the Movement, Black Power, and Black Economic Capacity
chapters.summary: In closing reflections Robinson emphasizes Ella Baker's simple plan — bring people from where they are to where they can be — as SNCC's enduring gift. He critiques Oprah Winfrey's Selma film as factually unreliable (the depicted Jim Forman / John Lewis car scene and the Forman / King exchanges 'never happened,' Robinson says: 'Jim was more of a leader than Martin'). He praises Phil Alden Robinson's Freedom Song for accurately depicting SNCC's Mississippi voter-registration work. On Black Power, Robinson argues the movement's intent was not guns: 'we knew we can't win with guns. The Indians didn't win with guns.' His vision is economic — Black entrepreneurs pooling capital to establish community financial institutions, laundromats, restaurants, and electronics manufacturing in small Mississippi towns like Greenwood (where 'Red Brown' had a denim manufacturing company called MACE). He cites Henry G. Parks Jr.'s Parks Sausage Company as a model of Black-owned business that originated from policy-book capital and built broad Baltimore enterprise. He laments contemporary Baltimore (post-Freddie Gray), Pikesville's stark divides, and the limits of voter registration alone. His closing position: he's available to organize wherever asked, except for piano lessons and dental appointments. He defines himself as a Free Black Person (FBP) since 1961.

errors: Transcript reference to 'Red Brown' (segment 2103) and a denim company called 'MACE' is preserved; this may refer to H. Rap Brown / Jamil Al-Amin or another individual. Without firmer identification the summary uses the transcript's name.
Henry G. Parks Jr. and Parks Sausage Co. detail is accurate per historical record.
improvements: Cross-check 'Red Brown' and the 'MACE' Greenwood manufacturing company against MFDP/SNCC economic-development records.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: SNCC Field Secretary; Cambridge Maryland Movement Organizer

evidence: Opens with chronological birth/family origin and Cortez Peters Business College entry (Segment 12, 00:00:56,000)
Smooth transition from Baltimore voter-registration practice to McComb voter-registration practice (Segment 128, 00:16:06,000)
Returns to economic-empowerment thesis at end as a closing reflective frame (Segment 2097, 03:21:25,000)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Robinson's narrative follows a clear chronological arc from Baltimore birth through SNCC service through post-SNCC career and ends with a reflective economic-empowerment thesis. Some backtracking and tangents (notably the Wyatt Tee Walker collection anecdote interrupting and resuming) but interviewer Emilye Crosby keeps the thread.

most_vivid_moment.quote: He pulls out a silver handle pistol and says don't worry about a thing. Sit tight.

evidence: Black eye-patch from pretzel injury before Louisville SNCC meeting (Segment 70, 00:08:40,000)
Reconstructed dialogue with the Magnolia jailer ('You want them know the niggas') (Segment 261, 00:29:20,000)
Tobacco-chewing jailer description with stair detail and three doors slamming (Segments 256-265, 00:28:40-00:30:01)
Sauna and natural mountain stream with Paul Robeson at Katonah (Segments 1893-1896, 03:01:14-03:01:28)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Exceptional concrete detail. Robinson reconstructs scenes with reconstructed dialogue, specific addresses (Burglund Supermarket, Auburn Avenue, Wall Street McComb), named individuals, and sensory details (silk pajamas in jail, silver-handled pistol, top hat and tails).

most_authentic_moment.quote: I almost forgot about the kids that I had with me. But that was about the one or only time that I had that kind of a fear.

vulnerability_elements: Admits the McComb sewer-workmen fear that almost made him forget the children he was canvassing with (Segment 196-203)
Discusses marriage breakdown and personal crisis with back pain, smoking, coffee (Segments 1404-1500)
Frank tensions with Bill Hansen articulated without diplomatic softening (Segments 428-441)
Acknowledges his draft 4-F status with ink-blot story (Segments 685-707)
emotional_authenticity.justification: Robinson is candid about his fears, his marriage's end, his complicated assessments of fellow SNCC workers, and his disagreements with leadership. Range from fear (sewer scene) to pride (Free Black Person identity) to humor (4-F draft story) to grief (movement losses) is wide and unguarded.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Robinson is a master oral-history narrator whose stories carry concrete detail and reconstructed dialogue. His structure is mostly chronological with effective thematic returns. Emotional access is broad and largely unguarded — he names personal failings and grievances openly. Occasional self-correction (June vs. July, name uncertainty) reads as integrity, not unreliability.

major_events: McComb voter registration with Bob Moses fall 1961 (Segments 167-228, 00:18:19-00:25:56)
Pike County student arrests and Magnolia jail visit (Segments 241-272, 00:26:54-00:31:01)
McComb high school walkout and Bob Moses beating (Segments 339-360, 00:41:54-00:46:00)
Cambridge Maryland movement and Maryland National Guard occupation 1962-64 (Segments 396-742, 00:52:43-01:32:04)
MFDP Atlantic City convention 1964 support work (Segments 1626-1632, 02:39:38-02:40:04)
Albany Movement jail-food delivery to King and Abernathy (Segments 1782-1801, 02:51:35-02:53:04)

key_figures.name: Gloria Richardson
key_figures.relationship: Cambridge Movement chair, working colleague with conflict

key_figures.name: Wyatt Tee Walker
key_figures.relationship: SCLC contact who repeatedly tried to recruit him

key_figures.name: Martin Luther King Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Delivered food to him in Albany jail

key_figures.name: Paul Robeson
key_figures.relationship: Met in sauna at Sam Rosen's Katonah home

organizational_involvement.role: Second in command, Center Number One

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Robinson is one of the few surviving SNCC field secretaries with direct experience opening the McComb voter-registration project alongside Bob Moses and Webb Owens, and the only one with detailed first-person experience of the SNCC Cambridge Maryland project at field-secretary level. His Marylander outsider position gave him a unique vantage on Nashville-Atlanta-NAG SNCC factional dynamics. His CDGM-Stennis confrontation, post-SNCC OSTI/Kaiser Oakland work, and DC government career mean he carries the SNCC organizational generation's trajectory into urban Black economic development.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Robinson sits at the field-secretary level in SNCC's two most consequential original projects: the McComb opening and the Cambridge Maryland sustained campaign. His insider access to SNCC executive leadership (McDew, Forman, Baker) and his cross-class contacts (Belafonte, Robeson, Kennedy, Stennis) make him a high-value first-person source.

precision_examples: June 1961 SNCC meeting in Louisville, Kentucky (Segments 76-77, 00:09:55-00:10:16)
Cortez Peters Business College three locations: Washington DC, Baltimore, Chicago (Segment 46, 00:04:41,000)
Maryland Adjutant General George Gelston, second in command Colonel Tawes the governor's nephew, then Colonel Boxer from Baltimore (Segments 655, 719, 735-738, 01:25:48-01:31:34)
verifiable_claims: Herbert Lee killed by State Representative E.H. Hurst in cotton gin (verified against civil_rights_facts.json)
Henry G. Parks Jr. Parks Sausage Co. (verifiable)
Wyatt Tee Walker SCLC executive director recruiting (consistent with Walker biography)
specificity_of_details.justification: Robinson provides specific years where he can ('61, '64, '65, '81, '01) and many full names with affiliations. Some uncertainty appears around dates ('June or July 61') but he flags it as uncertainty rather than asserting precisely. Many claims are independently verifiable.

new_information: First-person reconstruction of the Mama Cotton boarding-house network of McComb organizers' housing
Maryland public-accommodations referendum confrontation with Gloria Richardson over SNCC affiliate voting strategy (Segments 405-422, 01:08:48-01:09:21)
Details on Maryland National Guard General George Gelston as movement ally (Segments 655-742, 01:25:48-01:32:04)
Webb Owens 'Super Cool Daddy' as McComb logistical architect for SNCC (Segments 176-188, 00:21:06-00:22:15)
Personal recollection of meeting Paul Robeson in 1962-63 at Sam Rosen's Katonah home (Segments 1888-1897, 03:00:36-03:01:37)
understudied_aspects: Cambridge Maryland Movement field-secretary perspective (most existing literature focuses on Gloria Richardson)
Post-SNCC trajectory into urban Black community-economic-development organizations (UPO, OSTI, Kaiser Oakland)
Internal SNCC office politics around the Nashville-Atlanta-NAG factional rivalries
Maryland-as-border-state movement field operations vs. deep-South operations
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Robinson's account complicates the dominant narrative that SNCC's field secretaries were southern recruits — he is a Baltimore product whose movement origin was the CIG. His critique of Bill Hansen as 'hiding from his own community' challenges the standard SNCC interracial-cooperation story. His skeptical reading of Gloria Richardson's later trajectory ('she may have gotten a few things out of kilter') challenges the canonization of Richardson.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of the Cambridge Maryland Movement, McComb voter registration, the MFDP Atlantic City work, and the post-1965 SNCC diaspora into urban community development will find Robinson a primary witness with detailed, specific, sometimes idiosyncratic testimony. His Baltimore-CIG perspective on Greater SNCC factional politics is rare.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Substantial new material on Cambridge Maryland (a relatively understudied SNCC campaign), the McComb logistical network around Webb Owens and Mama Cotton, and the post-1965 SNCC career trajectory into Black community economic development. The Robeson Katonah encounter and the Stennis 'you boys' confrontation are unique and memorable.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Robinson is a high-historical-value source for at least three understudied corners of the SNCC story: the Cambridge Maryland Movement, the McComb logistical network, and the post-SNCC organizational diaspora into urban Black community economic development. His Baltimore-origin perspective adds a needed border-state lens to the dominant deep-South SNCC narrative.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: From a Baltimore high-school dropout into a SNCC field secretary, then through urban community-economic-development work, to a self-defined 'Free Black Person.' The arc explicitly traces the 1961 entry into SNCC as the moment Robinson sought to become independent — a goal he says he met by his 2001 retirement.
key_turning_points: Cousin Josephine Thorn convincing him to enroll at Cortez Peters (Segments 36-41, 00:03:24-00:04:02)
Walter Dixon sending him to represent Cortez Peters at college meetings (Segment 54, 00:06:02,000)
Ella Baker recognizing him on Anne Braden's porch (Segments 86-94, 00:11:35-00:12:27)
Confronting Senator Stennis at CDGM and losing the job (Segments 1267-1289, 02:10:27-02:11:21)
Walking out of the DC government job May 2001 (Segments 1582-1587, 02:36:25-02:36:44)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Robinson moved from a street-life Baltimore teenager with little defined direction to a man who closes the interview with a fully articulated 1961-set life mission ('to become a Free Black Person') that he claims to have lived out.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Robinson's clear articulation of long-arc personal mission setting ('I am a Free Black Person') and his economic-empowerment proposal for the next generation make this a structurally inspirational narrative.
transformation_growth.justification: The transformation arc is real and articulated, but Robinson tells it more as continuity of resolve than as conversion-type growth. Some specific turning points (Josephine Thorn, Ella Baker recognition, Stennis confrontation) are well-marked.

peak_moments.description: Visiting Curtis Hayes and Hollis Watkins in Magnolia jail with the tobacco-chewing hostile jailer
peak_moments.stakes: Could have been beaten or arrested by Mississippi jailer

peak_moments.description: Bob Moses beaten during McComb high school walkout while Robinson shields Diane Nash on the Masonic Temple roof
Confronting Senator John C. Stennis at CDGM about 'you boys'
Cliff Voss charging Maryland National Guard fixed bayonets in Cambridge, leading to tear gas across Black community
Black Panthers rejecting Robinson over Kaiser Engineers Cadillac

conflict_types: Personal-leadership disagreement (Gloria Richardson, Bill Hansen, Stennis)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From paralyzing fear (sewer workmen) to defiant righteousness (Stennis confrontation) to wry humor (4-F ink-blots) to grief (Herbert Lee, Louis Allen) to triumph (becoming an FBP)
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Multiple high-stakes moments are vividly described. Robinson's confrontations are dramatic and clear; his McComb experience carries the most physical danger; the Cambridge tear gas incident has palpable crisis tension.

universal_themes: Family pressure to find a path
Long-arc commitment to a personal mission
Mortality and physical decline (back pain crisis)
relatable_struggles: Dropping out of high school and finding direction
Job loss for principled refusal to accept disrespect
Caring for aging family while building a career
human_moments: Doodling hearts about Diane Nash at his first SNCC meeting (Segments 1029-1043, 01:54:55-01:55:38)
Mother's cousin Josephine intervening to redirect his life (Segments 36-41, 00:03:24-00:04:02)
His mother fixing lunch for Miss Hamer and Ella Baker (Segments 1860-1862, 02:57:19-02:57:36)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: The cousin-intervention origin, the long-arc personal-mission framing ('Free Black Person'), the late-career walkout from a bureaucratic job after a single 'this is bullshit' moment — all are recognizable human structures across diverse audiences.
relatable_experiences.justification: Robinson grounds his civil-rights story in family and mentorship structures that translate across audiences. The doodling-heart confession about Diane Nash and the mother's-lunch with Fannie Lou Hamer are vivid human moments. His somewhat specialized vocabulary (SNCC factional politics) and his Mississippi-specific stories occasionally limit relatability.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Robinson's emotional resonance is strong on the conflict/drama and transformation axes. Some specialized SNCC context occasionally distances general audiences, but the family and mentorship throughlines (Josephine Thorn, his mother, Ella Baker, Joanne Grant, Fannie Lou Hamer) and his closing economic-empowerment vision give the interview broad reach.

barriers: Heavy use of SNCC-specific names and acronyms (NAG, CIG, MFDP, CDGM, UPO, OSTI, VEP) often without explanation
Frequent name-only references to individuals listeners may not know (Webb Owens 'Super Cool Daddy', C.C. Bryant, Mama Cotton, Mr. Plevis)
Occasional ASR-driven name variants (Hitz/Hayes, Gaston) requiring listener inference
Robinson assumes movement-insider knowledge of the Atlantic City convention, Selma walks, and SNCC factional history
language_clarity.justification: Robinson's spoken language is articulate and natural; he tells stories well and contextualizes the bigger picture. He occasionally lapses into name-density without explanation that requires familiar listeners or extensive footnoting for general audiences.

slow_sections: Mid-career narrative through 1970s consultancies (02:13:00-02:25:00) — dense with consulting-job titles
Closing economic-empowerment thesis (02:55:35-03:25:00) — argumentative rather than narrative
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Sustained engagement throughout the 3.5-hour interview; some attention-flagging in the bureaucratic-career middle section, recovered by the closing reflection.
narrative_flow.justification: Emilye Crosby is an effective interviewer who keeps Robinson on track, prompts elaboration on key figures, and asks for context (Ella Baker mentorship, Cambridge dynamics) at the right moments. Robinson's natural storytelling rhythm carries most of the interview.

themes_connected: Generational handoff and the limits of voter registration alone
State violence (Baltimore Freddie Gray) connected to 1960s movement aftermath
Media representation of the movement (Selma film critique)
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Robinson explicitly connects his 1961-65 movement experience to 2015 Baltimore (Freddie Gray) and to the limits of post-Voting-Rights-Act electoral politics. His economic-empowerment thesis is explicitly framed as the unfinished work for the next generation.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Robinson is explicit and articulate about what his experience means in the present — he positions himself as still available to organize, names current cities needing help (Selma, Ferguson, Chicago), and offers a concrete proposal (community financial institutions, laundromats, manufacturing).
broader_themes_connection.justification: Excellent thematic connection. Robinson actively engages contemporary issues, contextualizes his historical experience in current Baltimore/Ferguson/Chicago, and proposes specific economic-empowerment programs as the movement's next chapter.

accessibility.overall_assessment: The interview is highly accessible for movement-familiar audiences and remains engaging for general audiences thanks to Robinson's storytelling skill and Crosby's facilitation. Some SNCC-specific jargon density requires either footnoting or interviewer prompting for full general access.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Reginald Robinson's Civil Rights History Project interview is a high-value field-secretary memoir of the McComb voter-registration opening, the Cambridge Maryland Movement, and the post-SNCC trajectory into Black community economic development. His storytelling is rich with reconstructed dialogue, named individuals, and concrete sensory detail. He is candid about internal SNCC conflicts, his disagreements with Gloria Richardson and Bill Hansen, his personal struggles, and his unfinished-agenda critique of post-Voting-Rights-Act movement politics. His Baltimore-origin Maryland perspective is a needed counter to the dominant deep-South SNCC narrative. His closing economic-empowerment vision — Black entrepreneurs pooling capital for community financial institutions, laundromats, manufacturing — frames his story as foreword to ongoing work, not closure.
key_strengths: Exceptional concrete detail with reconstructed dialogue, named individuals, and specific dates that make scenes vividly visualizable
Border-state field-secretary perspective (Baltimore-origin) that complicates the deep-South-only SNCC narrative

Direct insider testimony on McComb logistics (Webb Owens, Mama Cotton, Burglund Supermarket), Cambridge Movement dynamics (Gloria Richardson, General Gelston), and SNCC factional politics (Chuck McDew, James Forman, Ed King, Nashville-Atlanta-NAG rivalries)
Articulate present-tense engagement with contemporary issues and a concrete economic-empowerment program proposal that frames the interview as forward-looking, not retrospective
Candor about intra-movement conflicts, personal failings, and disagreements with iconic figures (Gloria Richardson, Bill Hansen, John Lewis selection)
key_limitations: Occasional ASR-related name uncertainty (Hitz vs. Hayes, 'A.G. Gaston of Detroit', the 'Cor Re' Oakland project) that requires careful editorial cross-checking
Heavy SNCC jargon and acronym density (NAG, CIG, MFDP, CDGM, UPO, OSTI, VEP) that limits general-audience accessibility without footnoting

Some self-acknowledged date uncertainty and tangential narrative threading that requires the interviewer to redirect; the middle-career bureaucratic stretch (1970s consultancies, 1980s DC employment work) slows the dramatic arc

claims.claim: Reginald 'Reggie' Robinson was born in Baltimore, Maryland, on October 2, 1939
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was born in Baltimore, Maryland, October 2, 1939, to Leroy and Leo Vesta, L-E-O-V-E-S-T-E-R Robinson (Segment 12, 00:00:56,000)

claims.claim: His parents were Leroy and Leo Vester Robinson; mother's maiden name was Jenkins
claims.supporting_excerpt: to Leroy and Leo Vesta, L-E-O-V-E-S-T-E-R Robinson... My mother's maiden name was Jenkins (Segments 12-13)

claims.claim: His father worked at Bethlehem Steel for 39 years and died one month after retiring
claims.supporting_excerpt: my dad worked at Bethlehem Steel for 39 years until he retired. And he died a month after he retired (Segments 22-23)

claims.claim: Robinson attended Dunbar High School through about tenth and a half grades before dropping out
claims.supporting_excerpt: I went to Dunbar High School until about the tenth and a half grades... I discovered that I didn't quite understand what French was going to do for me (Segments 29-30)

claims.claim: His cousin Josephine Thorn convinced him to enroll at Cortez Peters Business College
claims.supporting_excerpt: I have a cousin by the name of Josephine Thorn... She convinced my father, well she convinced me first that I needed to stop doing what I was doing... I enrolled into Cortez Peters Business College (Segments 36-41)

claims.claim: Walter Dixon was both the school dean and a Baltimore City Councilman who introduced a public accommodations bill
claims.supporting_excerpt: the dean of my school, Walter Dixon, was also the City Councilman in Baltimore who had just introduced a public accommodations bill (Segment 53)

claims.claim: Robinson became treasurer of the Civic Interest Group (CIG)
claims.supporting_excerpt: I became the treasurer of the civic interest group (Segment 65)

claims.claim: CIG voter registration concentrated in Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s area and registered about 30,000 people
claims.supporting_excerpt: it was concentrated in one general area which was Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s area. And we raised, oh I think it was about 30,000 people in a very short period of time (Segments 61-62)
claims.rationale: Direct match (with Robinson's own approximation marker).

claims.claim: Clarence Mitchell III stepped aside; his grandmother was president of the NAACP
claims.supporting_excerpt: Clarence's grandmother had said that the NAACP, she was president of the NAACP... Clarence had to make a decision (Segments 67-68)
claims.rationale: Transcript supports the claim but is ambiguous about whether 'president of the NAACP' means national or Baltimore branch. Historical record indicates Lillie Mae Carroll Jackson was president of the Baltimore NAACP branch, not the national organization.

claims.claim: Robinson attended the SNCC meeting in Louisville, Kentucky in June 1961
claims.supporting_excerpt: I go to the SNCC meeting in Louisville, Kentucky and July of, you know, I went in June of 61 (Segments 76-77)
claims.rationale: Robinson self-corrects to June; direct match after correction.

claims.claim: Ella Baker recognized Robinson on Anne Braden's porch at the Louisville meeting
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was standing on Anne Braden's porch drinking a beer by myself and this little lady walks up to me... This was Ella Baker (Segments 86-94)

claims.claim: Wyatt Tee Walker tried to recruit Robinson for SCLC in Atlanta
claims.supporting_excerpt: Oh, why do I like for you to meet our new field secretary?... And of course, when Ed went to someplace else and Ed and Ed and I had a private conversation, I wanted me to work for SCLC of course (Segments 152-157)
claims.rationale: Direct match (though transcript names this person 'YT' for Wyatt Tee Walker; both first-person identification and Walker's documented SCLC executive director role at the time confirm the identification).

claims.claim: Robinson met Bob Moses in McComb after a layover in Atlanta with Ed King (SNCC executive director)
claims.supporting_excerpt: When I get to Atlanta, I meet Ed King, who's our executive director at the time (Segment 144); ...And Ed got the word that I was not to go to the Delta, but to meet Bob Moses in McComb (Segment 166)
claims.rationale: Direct match. Ed King here refers to SNCC's Edward B. King Jr., not Rev. Edwin King of Mississippi.

claims.claim: George Hayes (transcript: 'Hitz'), a local McComb cab driver, drove Robinson to C.C. Bryant's house
claims.supporting_excerpt: At McComb, I was met by George Hitz, who was a local cab driver... George took me from the train station to CC Bryant's. I was with Bob Moses and CC was waiting for me (Segments 169-172)
claims.rationale: Transcript names 'George Hitz' which is likely a Whisper rendering of 'George Hayes,' the documented McComb SNCC cab driver. The factual claim (cab driver named George drove Robinson from station to CC Bryant) is fully supported; the spelling is uncertain.

claims.claim: Webb Owens, known locally as 'Super Cool Daddy,' was the McComb logistical architect for SNCC's setup
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was introduced to Mr. Webb Orange, who was better known as Super Cool Daddy. And I was told that Super Cool Daddy was the man to see, to do everything (Segments 176-179)
claims.rationale: Transcript reads 'Webb Orange' but this is consistent with documented McComb SNCC supporter 'Webb Owens' nicknamed Super Cool Daddy. The factual claim is supported; the name spelling has Whisper-introduced variation.

claims.claim: Robinson and a team set up a school over the Burglund Supermarket in the Masonic Temple
claims.supporting_excerpt: over top of Burglund High School, over top of Burglund Supermarket was the Messade Temple. We set up a school there (Segments 192-194)

claims.claim: Mississippi voter registration required interpreting the state Constitution; the registrar would flip pages and demand interpretation
claims.supporting_excerpt: the law of the state was, you had to interpret the Constitution of Mississippi in order to register the vote... he would take the Constitution and flip the pages and turn to any page in the book and say, write that, read that (Segments 207-211)

claims.claim: Herbert Lee was killed in the cotton gin by Mississippi State Representative E.H. Hurst
claims.supporting_excerpt: Herbert Lee got killed in the cotton gin by State Representative E.H. Hurst (Segments 220-221)
claims.rationale: Direct match and consistent with civil_rights_facts.json entry on Herbert Lee.

claims.claim: Robinson visited Curtis Hayes, Hollis Watkins (and others) in Magnolia jail dressed in suit and tie
claims.supporting_excerpt: Curtis and Hollis, Curtis Hayes and Hollis Watkins and Bobby Talbert and I think Brenda Travis... went down to the bus station I think it was and got arrested... So I got elected to go and visit them in the jail. They were down in Magnolia (Segments 242-248); ...I was into my black suit looking like a lawyer or a minister... I had my attitude case (Segment 254)

claims.claim: Bob Moses was beaten during the McComb high school walkout while Robinson was on the Masonic Temple roof shielding Diane Nash and 'A.G. Gaston of Detroit'
claims.supporting_excerpt: we left Diane Nash in the Masonic temple because we didn't want her to get get caught... I see the big sergeant walking up the stairs to the Masonic temple inside the Masonic temple is a guy from Detroit by the name of A.G. Gaston and Diane Nash... I told Diane Nash and Gaston come with me we got through the black space and open the door and where we were on the roof (Segments 348-357)
claims.rationale: Robinson's account is the source. Historically A.G. Gaston was a Birmingham businessman (not Detroit), so the transcript identification may conflate two individuals or contain a Whisper transcription artifact. The protective action on the roof is fully supported by transcript; the Gaston identification is uncertain.

claims.claim: Louis Allen was photographed by Robinson at a McComb mass meeting/funeral and was later murdered
claims.supporting_excerpt: I had the assignment of taking a picture of Louis Allen who I didn't know. But Dr. Anderson knew... And then later on of course Mr. Allen was killed (Segments 511-514)
claims.rationale: Direct match and consistent with civil_rights_facts.json entry on Louis Allen (murdered January 1964).

claims.claim: Amzie Moore convinced the McComb SNCC team to stay and hold a mass meeting/funeral
claims.supporting_excerpt: Then we had a long conversation with Amzie Moore. And Amzie Moore pointed out that we wanted to think that we had to do was to show that again that we were not afraid... So Amzy suggested that we have that the funeral become a mass meeting (Segments 505-508)

claims.claim: Robinson was arrested at a sit-in at Governor Tawes's restaurant in Crisfield, Maryland at Christmas 1961 with Faith Holsaert, Peggy Damman, Angela Butler, and Bill Hansen
claims.supporting_excerpt: There's Faith Holsaert there's I'm probably going to get a blinded kill steam and there's Peggy Dammon there's Angela Butler... we were joined by Bill Hansen... The intentions was to go to Crisfield, Maryland sit in at the governor and then Governor Tawes restaurant... This is at Christmas time 61 (Segments 370-373); ...this is the first and only time in the six years that I was with snake that I got locked up (Segment 378)

claims.claim: Freddie St. Clair's cousin Gloria Richardson took over leadership of the Cambridge movement
claims.supporting_excerpt: Freddie gave up the organization early on because not only Freddie was in business his dad was the funeral director... So then his cousin Gloria Richardson steps in (Segments 397-399)

claims.claim: Robinson opposed Gloria Richardson's plan to have Black voters boycott a public accommodations referendum
claims.supporting_excerpt: Gloria said that no black folks was going to vote for the referendum. I said, you can't do that... I says, no, you can't because you are an affiliate of snake. We got people dying in the South for road voter registration (Segments 407-414)

claims.claim: Maryland sent in National Guard Adjutant General George Gelston, who became a movement ally
claims.supporting_excerpt: they would send in the National Guard to squal this situation. Well, they sent in a general by the name of George Gelston. And George became an ally (Segments 654-657)

claims.claim: Robinson confronted Robert Kennedy in Cambridge about PT-9 tie clips
claims.supporting_excerpt: he was handing out there was a little PT 9 votes that his brother used to run. They were tire clips, right? So he said to me, he says, I would give you one, but you know where I tie. I said, well, your wife folks been hanging me long enough (Segments 580-582)
claims.rationale: Transcript carries some Whisper-induced ambiguity ('I tie' vs. 'I'd tie'); the gist of the Kennedy-Robinson confrontation is fully supported.

claims.claim: John Doar was a U.S. Justice Department attorney who interacted with SNCC's Mississippi voter-registration work and later supported Cambridge
claims.supporting_excerpt: There was Bob Moses Moses, John Doar, (Marion) Barry Barry, and me, and George Hedges (Segment 589); ...John Doar became a good friend, I mean, over the years (Segment 605); ...John Doar brought Mr. Hubbard in to Cambridge (Segment 607)
claims.rationale: Direct match; consistent with civil_rights_facts.json on John Doar.

claims.claim: Robinson did voter registration work in Orangeburg, South Carolina under the Voter Education Project (VEP)
claims.supporting_excerpt: when the voter education project came out, the VEP project came out... there was a little penny piece of money left for a project in Orangeburg, South Carolina (Segments 612-613)

claims.claim: Randolph Blackwell at VEP arranged Robinson's Orangeburg work through Dr. Thomas at South Carolina State
claims.supporting_excerpt: Randolph Blackwell, who was working with VEP at the time, who was also a professor in the HBC kind of situation. He got together, I think, with Dr. Thomas at State, South Carolina State (Segments 617-618)

claims.claim: Robinson took food to King and Abernathy at the Albany jail at Wyatt Tee Walker's request
claims.supporting_excerpt: YT came to me and said, come on, take a ride with me... He said, take this train into dock. And they had made the deal with the chief of police. There was no food that would come in to dock... him and Martin and Abanatha was in their cell. And they both had on a silk pajamas (Segments 1782-1794)
claims.rationale: Direct match (where YT = Wyatt Tee Walker, Abanatha = Abernathy).

claims.claim: Robinson worked for Senator Joseph D. Tydings before the 1964 Atlantic City convention
claims.supporting_excerpt: I left and went to work for Senator Joseph D. Tydings, Senator Joseph D. Tydings, just before the challenge (Segments 1616-1617)

claims.claim: Robinson lobbied California and Oregon for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party
claims.supporting_excerpt: Then I was assigned to go and lobby for the MFTP. I went to California, and I went to Oregon (Segments 1624-1625)
claims.rationale: Direct match. ('MFTP' is Robinson's verbal slip for 'MFDP'.)

claims.claim: Robinson and Mary Lovelace O'Neal of NAG worked support for MFDP in Atlantic City
claims.supporting_excerpt: Hooked up with Mary Lovelace O'Neal, who was from NAG, who was already on the job, trying to deal with this thing (Segments 1629-1630)

claims.claim: Robinson left SNCC in 1965 after Chuck McDew called him in
claims.supporting_excerpt: I left SNCC in 60, 65, D765. MacDoo was here in DC, and he was director of the United Planning Organization, center number one (Segments 1248-1252)

claims.claim: Robinson lost his Child Development Group of Mississippi job after challenging Senator John C. Stennis's 'you boys' address
claims.supporting_excerpt: we were at this meeting with, with one of Stenesis, Senator John C. Stennis, and this man just kept... can I say something, you boys?... why, is it that every time you get ready to say something to us, you've got to put some preference on about you boys, and a damn boy in this room... I had lost my job because you don't talk to Stenesis (Segments 1268-1286)
claims.rationale: Direct match; consistent with civil_rights_facts.json on John C. Stennis.

claims.claim: Robinson met Paul Robeson at Sam and Helen Rosen's home in Katonah, New York during a rest arranged by Dr. Joyce Baker of the Medical Committee for Human Rights
claims.supporting_excerpt: Ella suggested that I go talk to Joe Baker who was on the Medical Committee for Human Rights and she was a psychiatrist... we're going to send you to not mount Eagle. Peekskill. Peekskill in New York for about a week... And then got a surprise of being taken to Sam and Helen Roverson's house. Who Sam, Sam Roverson was the doctor that went to Russia to bring back Paul Robeson... this big black man comes out and sits next to me with this deep voice. And we introduce ourselves in damn for any Paul Robeson (Segments 1874-1892)
claims.rationale: Direct match. 'Roverson' is Whisper rendering of 'Rosen'; 'Joe Baker' likely 'Joyce Baker'. The Sam Rosen-Robeson historical relationship is documented.

claims.claim: Robinson got married at Harry Belafonte's home with his mother and Ella Baker in attendance
claims.supporting_excerpt: When I got married the night before I got married Ella and Joanne Grant took me out and got me drunk... And then I of course got married at Harry Belafonte's house. And of course my mother was there and of course Ella and my mother (Segments 1863-1866)

claims.claim: Robinson worked for the DC government for twenty years to the day, retiring in May 2001 at age 62
claims.supporting_excerpt: I left the government 20 years to the day... I was 62 years old. I was at May. And I came in in May 61. And I left May 2001 (Segments 1549-1586)
claims.rationale: Direct match (where '61' here means May 1981 per Robinson's earlier 'I've been back here in DC since 81' Segment 1516).

claims.claim: Robinson defines himself as a 'Free Black Person' (FBP) since 1961
claims.supporting_excerpt: In 1961, it's become a free black person. And if you notice my emails, I come on my last name, the red comma, FBP. And I'm dead serious about it. I am a free black person (Segments 1588-1591)

publication_decision.rationale: Main summary scores 86 accuracy / 87 quality — both fall short of the 90/90 Smithsonian publication threshold. Citation audit finds 4 partially_supported claims out of 39: (1) Clarence Mitchell III's grandmother's NAACP presidency scope (Baltimore vs. national); (2) George Hayes/Hitz spelling uncertainty driven by Whisper ASR; (3) Webb Owens/Orange spelling uncertainty; (4) 'A.G. Gaston of Detroit' identification conflict with the historically documented Birmingham A.G. Gaston. Zero unsupported claims, all major historical assertions are verified against the transcript, and several are cross-confirmed by the ground-truth civil_rights_facts.json corpus (Herbert Lee, Louis Allen, John C. Stennis, Bob Moses, Ella Baker, John Doar, Wyatt Tee Walker, Diane Nash, James Forman, Amzie Moore, Charles Sherrod, Bernard Lafayette, Stokely Carmichael, Julian Bond, Harry Belafonte). This summary should go to the human review queue where the four partial-support items can be resolved by an editor cross-checking the original audio for the Whisper-driven name variants and clarifying the Baltimore vs. national NAACP presidency. Once those four items resolve, the summary is publication-grade.
